whole_response:
The sequent  

\[
\forall x\,\exists y\,(P(x)\lor Q(y))\;\vdash\; \exists y\,\forall x\,(P(x)\lor Q(y))
\]

is **not derivable** in standard first‑order natural deduction.  
A counterexample can be given with a domain containing two elements \(a,b\) and the interpretation  

* \(P(a)\) true, \(P(b)\) false;  
* \(Q(a)\) false, \(Q(b)\) true.

Then \(\forall x\,\exists y\,(P(x)\lor Q(y))\) holds (for each \(x\) we can pick a suitable \(y\)), but there is no single \(y\) that works for all \(x\), so \(\exists y\,\forall x\,(P(x)\lor Q(y))\) fails. Hence no natural‑deduction proof exists.
